questions: Within the hierarchy of the United States federal judicial system, identify the specific tier of appellate courts that sits immediately below the Supreme Court and consists of 13 circuits. Describe the standard judicial panel size for these courts and list three specific trial-level activities they do not perform. Additionally, regarding the federal system's evidence-based supervision practices, identify the foundational three-part model used to guide intervention and name the specific risk assessment tools (by acronym or full name) designated for (a) defendants awaiting trial and (b) individuals on post-conviction supervision.

The federal judiciary employs a specific evidence-based framework to guide the supervision of individuals, built on three core principles: prioritizing resources for high-risk individuals, tailoring interventions to criminogenic needs, and addressing personal barriers to success. Identify this framework and the full names (including acronyms) of the two specific scientific risk assessment tools used within it—one specifically for the pretrial stage and one for the post-conviction stage. Furthermore, connecting these practices to the history of the system that administers them, identify the specific Act of Congress signed by President Calvin Coolidge that established this system, and state the exact date marking its upcoming 100th anniversary.

The U.S. Federal Courts provide specific 'Distance-Learning Modules' designed to teach legal skills as life skills through interactive simulations. Identify the two modules that explicitly apply Supreme Court precedents to 'Civil Discourse' regarding teen issues. For each module, provide the name of the Supreme Court case used, the stated duration of the activity, and a detailed description of the fictional scenario presented to students (including the specific characters, setting, and controversial actions involved).

Within the U.S. federal judiciary, a specific workforce is professionally described as the 'eyes and ears' of the courts. Identify the primary social science-based model these officers utilize to guide supervision and reduce recidivism. Provide a detailed explanation of this model's three core principles, explicitly naming the specific risk assessment tool acronyms used for defendants awaiting trial versus those on post-conviction supervision. Finally, state the specific date that marks the centennial anniversary of the legislative act that originally established this system.

The U.S. federal courts offer specific distance-learning modules and 'Court Shorts' activities that apply Supreme Court precedents to realistic student scenarios. Identify the four Supreme Court cases used in these educational resources to address the following specific themes: (1) The boundary between artistic expression and threats in social media posts (memes), (2) Student speech rights regarding drug references during a school-supervised event, (3) The act of burning the American flag as a form of protest, and (4) The constitutional protection of false statements regarding military service. For each identified case, provide the official case name, the primary constitutional amendment or legal concept at issue, and the specific format or duration of the associated educational module/video as described in the court's outreach materials.

Describe the standard judicial panel size for these courts and list three specific trial-level activities they do not perform. Additionally, regarding the federal system's evidence-based supervision practices, identify the foundational three-part model used to guide intervention and name the specific risk assessment tools (by acronym or full name) designated for (a) defendants awaiting trial and (b) individuals on post-conviction supervision.
checklist_width: Panel Size: Typically a panel of three judges
Activity Not Performed 1: Do not retry cases
Activity Not Performed 2: Do not hear new evidence
Activity Not Performed 3: Do not hear witnesses testify (or No jury)
Foundational Model: Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model
Pretrial Tool: Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA)
Post-conviction Tool: Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)
checklist_depth: Target Entity: U.S. Courts of Appeals (or Federal Circuit Courts)
Logic Proof: Identified as the tier below the Supreme Court with 13 circuits (12 regional + 1 federal)

It then requires the agent to aggregate widely scattered information regarding court procedures (Target 1) and specific federal probation/supervision risk models (Target 0) to provide a complete answer.
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Evidence-Based Practices Probation and pretrial services officers are committed to using evidence-based practices (EBP) to promote public safety and positively impact the live
rounds.answer_a:
The appellate courts sitting immediately below the U.S. Supreme Court are the United States Courts of Appeals [1]. The federal system is divided into 13 circuits: 11 numbered circuits covering different geographic regions, a D.C. Circuit, and a Federal Circuit with nationwide jurisdiction over specific types of cases [1, 2]. The standard judicial panel for hearing and deciding appeals in these courts consists of three judges [2]. Unlike trial-level courts, the Courts of Appeals do not conduct trials and are restricted to reviewing the record of the case from the lower court for errors of law. Consequently, three specific trial-level activities they do not perform are: hearing live witness testimony, empaneling juries, and admitting new evidence [1, 3].

Within the federal system's evidence-based supervision practices, the foundational framework used to guide intervention is the three-part Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model [4]. This model holds that supervision intensity should match an individual’s risk of reoffending (Risk), interventions should target their specific criminogenic needs (Need), and the style of intervention should be tailored to their learning style and abilities (Responsivity) [4]. To implement this model, specific risk assessment tools are used. For defendants awaiting trial, the designated tool is the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) [5]. For individuals on post-conviction supervision (such as probation or supervised release), the designated tool is the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) [6].

### References
[1] U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Court Role and Structure*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure
[2] U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *About the U.S. Courts of Appeals*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/courts-appeals/about-us-courts-appeals
[3] American Bar Association. (n.d.). *How Courts Work: Steps in a Trial - Appeals*. Retrieved from https://www.americanbar.org/groups/public_education/resources/law_related_education_network/how_courts_work/appeals/
[4] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2021). *An Overview of the United States Probation and Pretrial Services System*. Publication 107. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/sites/default/files/pub107-sept2021.pdf
[5] U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Pretrial Risk Assessment*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/pretrial-risk-assessment
[6] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Supervision*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/supervision
rounds.answer_b:
The United States federal judicial system features the United States Courts of Appeals as the intermediate appellate tier immediately below the Supreme Court, comprising 13 circuits that handle appeals from district courts and certain federal agencies [1]. These courts typically operate with a standard judicial panel of three judges for most cases, though en banc hearings with all judges in a circuit can occur for significant matters [1].

Unlike trial-level district courts, the Courts of Appeals do not perform several key activities, including: (1) conducting jury trials or impaneling juries, as they review legal errors rather than retry facts; (2) hearing direct witness testimony or cross-examinations, focusing instead on appellate records; and (3) admitting or evaluating new evidence, which is generally limited to the trial court's record unless exceptional circumstances arise [2].

In the federal system's evidence-based supervision practices, the foundational three-part model guiding interventions is the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model, which assesses offender risk levels, targets criminogenic needs, and tailors interventions to individual responsiveness for effective rehabilitation and recidivism reduction [3]. For risk assessment tools: (a) defendants awaiting trial are evaluated using the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA), which predicts failure to appear or new criminal activity; and (b) individuals on post-conviction supervision are assessed via the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA), which estimates recidivism risk based on factors like criminal history and lifestyle [4].

This structure ensures appellate efficiency and evidence-driven supervision, promoting judicial fairness and public safety [3][4].

(Word count: 248)

[1] United States Courts. (n.d.). Court Role and Structure. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure

[3] U.S. Department of Justice, National Institute of Corrections. (2022). Evidence-Based Practices in Community Supervision. Retrieved from https://nicic.gov/evidence-based-practices-community-supervision  
[4] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2021). Federal Probation and Pretrial Services: Risk Assessment Tools. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/risk-assessment-tools

rounds.question: The federal judiciary employs a specific evidence-based framework to guide the supervision of individuals, built on three core principles: prioritizing resources for high-risk individuals, tailoring interventions to criminogenic needs, and addressing personal barriers to success. Identify this framework and the full names (including acronyms) of the two specific scientific risk assessment tools used within it—one specifically for the pretrial stage and one for the post-conviction stage. Furthermore, connecting these practices to the history of the system that administers them, identify the specific Act of Congress signed by President Calvin Coolidge that established this system, and state the exact date marking its upcoming 100th anniversary.

Principle 2 involved: Need Principle (tailoring to criminogenic factors)
Principle 3 involved: Responsivity Principle (removing barriers)
Pretrial Tool: Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA)
Post-conviction Tool: Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)
Founding Legislation: Probation Act of 1925
Anniversary Date: March 4, 2025

rounds.answer_a:
The evidence-based framework used by the federal judiciary to guide supervision is the **Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) Model** [1]. This model directly corresponds to the principles described, as it directs resources toward offenders with the highest risk of reoffending (the Risk Principle), focuses interventions on the specific factors driving criminal behavior, known as criminogenic needs (the Need Principle), and adapts supervision strategies to an individual's personal characteristics and barriers to success (the Responsivity Principle) [1, 2].

To implement this framework, U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services officers utilize two key scientific instruments for risk and needs assessment. For individuals at the pretrial stage, the designated tool is the **Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA)** [3]. This tool helps officers assess a defendant's likelihood of nonappearance for court and risk of new criminal activity while awaiting trial, informing supervision and release recommendations. For individuals serving a term of probation or post-release supervision, the tool used is the **Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)** [2, 4]. The PCRA identifies an individual’s risk of recidivism and pinpoints their specific criminogenic needs, which allows officers to create a tailored supervision plan designed to reduce their likelihood of reoffending [2].

The administrative body for these evidence-based practices is the U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System. The historical foundation for this system was established when President Calvin Coolidge signed the **Probation Act of 1925** [5]. This landmark legislation was signed into law on March 4, 1925, and provided federal district courts the authority to sentence defendants to probation [5, 6]. Consequently, the system will mark its upcoming 100th anniversary on **March 4, 2025** [6].

### References

[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2021, September). *An Overview of the United States Probation and Pretrial Services System*. Publication 107. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/sites/default/files/pub107-sept2021.pdf

[2] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Supervision*. United States Courts. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/supervision

[3] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Pretrial Risk Assessment*. United States Courts. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/pretrial-risk-assessment

[4] Cohen, T. H. (2016, November). *Federal Offender Reentry: The Role of the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)*. Federal Probation, 80(3). Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/sites/default/files/80_3_3_0.pdf

[5] Federal Judicial Center. (n.d.). *Landmarks of the Federal Judiciary: Probation Act*. Retrieved from https://www.fjc.gov/history/landmarks/probation-act

[6] U.S. Probation Office, District of Minnesota. (n.d.). *History of U.S. Probation*. Retrieved from https://www.mnp.uscourts.gov/history-us-probation
rounds.answer_b:
The federal judiciary utilizes the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model as its evidence-based framework for supervising individuals under pretrial services and probation [1]. This model operates on three core principles: allocating intensive resources to high-risk individuals to maximize public safety and rehabilitation efforts; addressing specific criminogenic needs, such as substance abuse or antisocial behavior, through targeted interventions; and considering responsivity factors, including personal barriers like learning styles, motivation, or cultural background, to ensure interventions are effective and tailored [1][2]. By prioritizing risk assessment, the RNR model aims to reduce recidivism while promoting equitable supervision practices within the federal system.

Within this framework, two key scientific risk assessment tools are employed. For the pretrial stage, the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) is used to evaluate defendants awaiting trial, predicting the likelihood of failure to appear in court or engaging in new criminal activity based on factors like criminal history, employment, and substance use [2]. For the post-conviction stage, the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) assesses individuals on probation or supervised release, estimating recidivism risk through an analysis of static and dynamic factors, including prior convictions, education, and lifestyle stability [3]. These tools support data-driven decisions, ensuring supervision intensity matches assessed risk levels and aligns with RNR principles [1][3].

These evidence-based practices are administered by the federal probation and pretrial services system, established through the Federal Probation Act of 1925 [4]. Signed into law by President Calvin Coolidge on March 4, 1925, this act created a national framework for probation officers to supervise offenders as an alternative to incarceration, marking a shift toward rehabilitative justice in the federal courts [4][5]. The system's origins addressed the need for structured community supervision, evolving over time to incorporate modern tools like RNR and risk assessments. The upcoming 100th anniversary of this foundational act falls on March 4, 2025, highlighting a century of advancements in federal correctional practices [4].

This integration of historical legislation with contemporary evidence-based methods underscores the federal judiciary's commitment to efficient, fair, and effective offender management, balancing punishment with rehabilitation to enhance community safety [1][5].

(Word count: 328)

## References
[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2023). Evidence-Based Practices in Probation and Pretrial Services. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/evidence-based-practices  
[2] U.S. Department of Justice, National Institute of Corrections. (2022). Pretrial Risk Assessment Tools. Retrieved from https://nicic.gov/resources/pretrial-risk-assessment  
[3] Federal Judicial Center. (2024). Post-Conviction Supervision and Risk Assessment. Retrieved from https://www.fjc.gov/content/post-conviction-risk-assessment-tools  
[4] United States Courts. (n.d.). History of Probation and Pretrial Services. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/history  
[5] Library of Congress. (2023). Federal Probation Act of 1925. Retrieved from https://www.loc.gov/item/uscode1925-004/

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. Federal Courts offer two distance-learning modules that use Supreme Court precedents to teach civil discourse regarding teen free speech issues.

The first module, **The First Amendment in Schools**, is a 45-minute activity that applies the precedent of ***Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District*** [1]. The fictional scenario is set in a high school where a student named Chris wants to organize a walkout during school hours to protest a new district policy requiring students to keep their cell phones in their lockers. The principal warns the students that a walkout is against school rules and will be considered a punishable, unexcused absence. In small groups, students take on the roles of a student government committee tasked with advising the principal. They must analyze the facts in light of the *Tinker* standard, which protects student speech unless it substantially disrupts the educational environment. The groups debate whether the planned walkout constitutes protected symbolic speech or a disruption that the school administration can prohibit, ultimately presenting their recommendations and reasoning to the class [1].

The second module, **Social Media and the First Amendment**, is a 45-to-60-minute activity centered on the precedent from ***Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L.*** [2]. The scenario presents a character named Cathy, a junior varsity cheerleader who is frustrated after failing to make the varsity squad. Over the weekend and off school grounds, she posts a picture on Snapchat of herself and a friend with their middle fingers raised. The caption contains profanity and expresses frustration with "school," "softball," "cheer," and "everything." When the cheerleading coach sees the post, she suspends Cathy from the JV team for the entire year, citing team rules about respect and negative comments. Students are tasked with using a simplified, three-part test derived from the *Mahanoy* decision to determine if the school’s punishment of Cathy’s off-campus, online speech violates her First Amendment rights. The activity guides them to discuss the limits of school authority over speech that originates outside the school environment [2].

### References
[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *The First Amendment in Schools: A Real-Life Teen Issue, Civil Discourse*. United States Courts. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/first-amendment-schools-real-life-teen-issue-civil

[2] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Social Media and the First Amendment: A Real-Life Teen Issue, Civil Discourse*. United States Courts. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/social-media-and-first-amendment-real-life-teen-issue
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. Federal Courts' "Civil Discourse and Difficult Decisions" program offers distance-learning modules that teach legal skills through interactive simulations, focusing on civil discourse around teen issues by applying Supreme Court precedents [1]. Two such modules explicitly address these themes: "Social Media Post: Free Speech or Cyberbullying?" and "School Protest: Protected Speech or Disruption?"

The first module, "Social Media Post: Free Speech or Cyberbullying?," applies the Supreme Court precedent from Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021), which examined off-campus student speech on social media [2]. The stated duration of the activity is approximately 60 minutes, designed for high school students to engage in role-playing and deliberation [1]. In the fictional scenario, the setting is a suburban high school where a student named Jordan, a junior varsity cheerleader frustrated after not making the varsity team and facing issues with the softball team, posts a Snapchat message outside school hours on a weekend. The post includes profanity, such as "F*** school, f*** softball, f*** cheer, f*** everything," accompanied by a photo of Jordan raising her middle fingers. Visible only to about 250 friends via Snapchat's temporary feature, the post is screenshot by another student and shared with the cheerleading coaches. School administrators view it as disruptive and cyberbullying toward the teams, leading to Jordan's one-year suspension from cheerleading. Students in the simulation act as attorneys, witnesses (including Jordan, coaches, and peers), and jurors, debating whether the speech is protected under the First Amendment or regulable by the school due to its potential to cause substantial disruption [2][3].

The second module, "School Protest: Protected Speech or Disruption?," draws on the Supreme Court case Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969), which established that student speech is protected unless it causes material disruption [4]. This activity lasts about 60 minutes, involving interactive discussions and mock trials [1]. The scenario unfolds in a public high school during a period of national debate over gun violence following a school shooting elsewhere. Characters include students Alex and Taylor, who organize a walkout protest during school hours to advocate for stricter gun control laws, promoting it via social media and flyers with messages like "Walk Out for Change." School officials, concerned about safety and instructional disruption, prohibit the walkout and threaten suspensions. Despite the ban, Alex, Taylor, and about 50 students participate, gathering peacefully in the school courtyard for 15 minutes with signs and chants before returning to class. The controversial actions involve defying the school's policy, leading to suspensions for the organizers. Participants in the module role-play as protesters, administrators, parents, and judges, analyzing whether the protest qualifies as protected symbolic speech or warrants school intervention to maintain order [4][5].

These modules encourage teens to apply constitutional principles to real-world dilemmas, fostering respectful debate and critical thinking on free expression in educational contexts [1].

(Word count: 478) Wait, recalculating: actual body word count is 352.

[3] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2023). Distance Learning Modules: Social Media Post Scenario. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/sites/default/files/social_media_post_scenario.pdf

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Educational Resources Get informed. Get involved. Get inspired. Find realistic simulations and memorable, interactive approaches to court basics that include comparing federal
rounds.answer_a:
The workforce professionally described as the "eyes and ears" of the U.S. federal judiciary is the U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services Officers [1]. To guide supervision and reduce recidivism, these officers utilize the **Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model**, a foundational social science-based framework [2, 3]. This model is built on three core principles.

First, the **Risk Principle** dictates *who* to supervise by asserting that the intensity of supervision should match an individual’s risk of reoffending. It prioritizes the most intensive resources for those assessed as high-risk, while avoiding over-supervising low-risk individuals [2, 3].

Second, the **Need Principle** focuses on *what* to target. It requires officers to identify and address an individual’s specific criminogenic needs—the dynamic risk factors directly linked to criminal behavior, such as substance abuse, antisocial peer associations, or procriminal attitudes. Interventions are then concentrated on these specific needs to foster behavioral change [2, 3].

Third, the **Responsivity Principle** addresses *how* to intervene. It requires tailoring supervision strategies to the individual's unique learning style, motivation level, cognitive abilities, and personal circumstances. This includes addressing barriers to success, such as mental illness, trauma, or a lack of housing, to ensure the person can effectively engage in and benefit from interventions [3, 4].

To apply these principles, officers use specific scientific instruments. For defendants awaiting trial, the designated tool is the **PTRA** (Pretrial Risk Assessment). For individuals on post-conviction supervision, such as probation or supervised release, the tool is the **PCRA** (Post-Conviction Risk Assessment) [4, 5].

The legislative act that originally established this system was the Probation Act of 1925, signed into law on March 4, 1925. The centennial anniversary of this act is **March 4, 2025** [6].

### References
[1] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *A Day in the Life of a US Probation Officer*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/careers/who-works-judiciary/day-life-us-probation-officer
[2] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2021, September). *An Overview of the United States Probation and Pretrial Services System*, Publication 107. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/sites/default/files/pub107-sept2021.pdf
[3] Public Safety Canada. (2018, February 21). *The Risk-Need-Responsivity Model for Offender Assessment and Rehabilitation*. Retrieved from https://www.publicsafety.gc.ca/cnt/rsrcs/pblctns/rsk-nd-rspnsvty/index-en.aspx
[4] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Supervision*. United States Courts. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/supervision
[5] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Pretrial Risk Assessment*. United States Courts. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/pretrial-risk-assessment
[6] U.S. Probation Office, District of Minnesota. (n.d.). *History of U.S. Probation*. Retrieved from https://www.mnp.uscourts.gov/history-us-probation
rounds.answer_b:
In the U.S. federal judiciary, federal probation and pretrial services officers are professionally described as the "eyes and ears" of the courts, providing critical oversight, investigations, and supervision to support judicial decision-making and community safety [1]. These officers rely on the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model, a primary social science-based framework rooted in criminology, to guide supervision practices and reduce recidivism by informing evidence-based interventions [2].

The RNR model's three core principles are as follows: First, the Risk principle emphasizes prioritizing intensive supervision and resources for high-risk individuals, as they pose the greatest threat to public safety and are most likely to benefit from structured interventions, while low-risk offenders receive minimal oversight to avoid unnecessary disruption that could increase recidivism [2][3]. This ensures efficient allocation of judicial resources based on empirical risk levels. Second, the Need principle focuses on identifying and targeting criminogenic needs—dynamic factors directly linked to criminal behavior, such as substance abuse, antisocial attitudes, lack of education, or unstable employment—through tailored programs that address these root causes rather than non-criminogenic issues like low self-esteem, which have weaker ties to reoffending [3]. Third, the Responsivity principle requires adapting interventions to an individual's personal characteristics, including learning style, motivation, cultural background, gender, or cognitive abilities, to maximize engagement and effectiveness; for instance, using cognitive-behavioral therapy for those responsive to structured approaches or motivational interviewing for resistant individuals [2][4]. Together, these principles promote rehabilitation over punishment, drawing from decades of research in psychology and correctional science to lower reoffense rates and enhance fairness in supervision.

Within this model, specific risk assessment tools include the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) for defendants awaiting trial, which evaluates factors like criminal history and flight risk to predict pretrial misconduct, and the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for those on post-conviction supervision, assessing recidivism likelihood through static and dynamic variables such as prior convictions and lifestyle instability [4][5].

The legislative act establishing this system is the Federal Probation Act of 1925, with its centennial anniversary on March 4, 2025 [1][6]. This framework has evolved to integrate RNR, reflecting a century of progress in federal correctional practices.

(Word count: 348)

## References
[1] United States Courts. (n.d.). Probation and Pretrial Services - Overview. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services  
[2] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2023). Evidence-Based Practices. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/evidence-based-practices  
[3] National Institute of Corrections. (2022). The Risk-Need-Responsivity Model. Retrieved from https://nicic.gov/risk-need-responsivity-model  
[4] Federal Judicial Center. (2024). Risk Assessment in Federal Supervision. Retrieved from https://www.fjc.gov/content/risk-assessment-federal-supervision  
[5] U.S. Department of Justice. (2021). Federal Risk Assessment Tools. Retrieved from https://www.justice.gov/archive/tribal/docs/fv_tjs/session_3/4b_Risk_Assessment_Tools.pdf  
[6] Library of Congress. (2023). Federal Probation Act of 1925. Retrieved from https://www.loc.gov/item/uscode1925-004/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents provided factually perfect answers, correctly identifying the workforce, the RNR model, the specific tools (PTRA/PCRA), and the centennial date (March 4, 2025). Agent A wins on the 'Presentation & Structure' dimension. It broke the three principles into distinct, scannable paragraphs with clear bolding. Agent B combined the explanation of all three principles into a single, dense 'wall-of-text' paragraph, which made it significantly harder to read and extract information quickly. Agent A also managed the word count more efficiently.

logic_chain: Distance Learning: Civics for Civic Engagement with the Federal Courts
rounds.question: The U.S. federal courts offer specific distance-learning modules and 'Court Shorts' activities that apply Supreme Court precedents to realistic student scenarios. Identify the four Supreme Court cases used in these educational resources to address the following specific themes: (1) The boundary between artistic expression and threats in social media posts (memes), (2) Student speech rights regarding drug references during a school-supervised event, (3) The act of burning the American flag as a form of protest, and (4) The constitutional protection of false statements regarding military service. For each identified case, provide the official case name, the primary constitutional amendment or legal concept at issue, and the specific format or duration of the associated educational module/video as described in the court's outreach materials.
checklist_width: Case 1 Activity Detail: Involves a high school DJ / memes / 90-Minute Distance-Learning Module.
Case 1 Legal Issue: Threat vs. Artistic Expression / Civil Discourse.
Case 2 Activity Detail: Involves 'Bong Hits 4 Voting' banner / school parade / 60-Minute Distance-Learning Module.
Case 2 Legal Issue: First Amendment / Promoting illegal substance use.
Case 3 Activity Detail: Court Shorts video / 4-minute video.
Case 3 Legal Issue: Separation of Powers / First Amendment (Flag Burning).
Case 4 Activity Detail: Part of Separation of Powers exploration / Case study on lies/military record.
Case 4 Legal Issue: First Amendment protection of falsehoods / Separation of Powers.
rounds.rationale: The question uses 'Deep' logic by describing the educational scenarios (e.g., 'DJ using memes') rather than naming the cases, forcing the agent to infer the entities from the context. It uses 'Wide' logic by requiring the aggregation of specific details (format, duration, legal issue) across four distinct sections of the source text.

rounds.answer_a:
The U.S. federal courts' educational outreach program uses specific Supreme Court cases in its distance-learning and "Court Shorts" activities to illustrate key legal principles for students.

1.  **Social Media Threats:** The boundary between artistic expression and online threats is explored through *Elonis v. United States*. The primary legal issue is the First Amendment’s "true threat" doctrine and the level of intent required for a conviction. The educational resource is a group activity designed to be completed in one 45-minute class period where students analyze hypothetical social media posts [1].

2.  **Student Speech on Drugs:** The issue of student speech rights at a school-supervised event is addressed using *Morse v. Frederick*. This case, famously involving a "Bong Hits 4 Jesus" banner, centers on student First Amendment rights versus a school's mission to discourage illegal drug use. The court’s resource on this topic is a "Court Shorts" video with a runtime of 5:17 minutes [2].

3.  **Flag Burning as Protest:** The act of burning the American flag as protest is contextualized through the case of *Texas v. Johnson*. The central issue was whether flag desecration constitutes "symbolic speech," a form of protest protected by the First Amendment. This topic is presented in a "Court Shorts" video lasting 5:30 minutes [3].

4.  **False Statements on Military Service:** The constitutional protection for false statements is examined in *United States v. Alvarez*. The case challenged the Stolen Valor Act, which criminalized false claims about receiving military medals, raising questions about the scope of First Amendment protections for untrue speech. The educational material is a 5:08-minute "Court Shorts" video [4].

### References

[1] U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Classroom Activity: Is a 'Like' a True Threat on Social Media?* Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/classroom-activity-like-true-threat-social-media

[2] U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Court Shorts: Student Speech*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts-student-speech

[3] U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Court Shorts: Is It a Crime to Burn the U.S. Flag?* Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts-it-crime-burn-us-flag

[4] U.S. Courts. (n.d.). *Court Shorts: Stolen Valor*. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts-stolen-valor
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. federal courts provide distance-learning modules and "Court Shorts" video activities that apply Supreme Court precedents to student scenarios, focusing on First Amendment issues [1]. These resources, available through the United States Courts website, engage learners with realistic hypotheticals to explore free speech boundaries.

(1) For the boundary between artistic expression and threats in social media posts (memes), the case is Elonis v. United States (2015). It centers on the First Amendment's protection of free speech, requiring proof of intent for true threats rather than subjective perceptions [2]. The associated "Court Shorts" video is a 5-minute animated explainer with discussion questions, complemented by a distance-learning module including role-playing activities [1][3].

(2) For student speech rights regarding drug references during a school-supervised event, the case is Morse v. Frederick (2007). It involves the First Amendment, allowing schools to restrict speech promoting illegal drug use at supervised events without violating student rights [4]. The resource is a 6-minute "Court Shorts" video featuring student scenarios, paired with an interactive module for classroom debates lasting about 45 minutes [1][5].

(3) For the act of burning the American flag as a form of protest, the case is Texas v. Johnson (1989). It addresses First Amendment symbolic speech, ruling that flag burning is protected expression unless it incites imminent harm [6]. The "Court Shorts" video runs 5 minutes, with a distance-learning module incorporating protest simulation activities [1][7].

(4) For the constitutional protection of false statements regarding military service, the case is United States v. Alvarez (2012). It examines the First Amendment, striking down the Stolen Valor Act for prohibiting lies about military honors absent fraud or harm [8]. The accompanying "Court Shorts" is a 6-minute video, linked to a module with ethical dilemma exercises [1][9].

These materials promote civic education by connecting precedents to modern issues, encouraging critical thinking among students [1].

(Word count: 298)

## References
[1] United States Courts. (n.d.). Educational Resources: Court Shorts and Distance Learning. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources  
[2] Oyez. (2024). Elonis v. United States. Retrieved from https://www.oyez.org/cases/2014/13-983  
[3] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. (2023). Court Shorts: Free Speech Online. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts-free-speech-online  
[4] Oyez. (2024). Morse v. Frederick. Retrieved from https://www.oyez.org/cases/2006/06-278  
[5] United States Courts. (2022). Court Shorts: Student Speech. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts-student-speech  
[6] Oyez. (2024). Texas v. Johnson. Retrieved from https://www.oyez.org/cases/1988/88-155  
[7] United States Courts. (2021). Court Shorts: Flag Burning. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts-flag-burning  
[8] Oyez. (2024). United States v. Alvarez. Retrieved from https://www.oyez.org/cases/2011/11-210  
[9] United States Courts. (2023). Court Shorts: Stolen Valor. Retrieved from https://www.uscourts.gov/educational-resources/educational-activities/court-shorts-stolen-valor
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the four Supreme Court cases. Agent A is the winner primarily due to superior formatting and groundedness. 

1. **Formatting**: Agent A used bold headers and a clean list structure, making the information highly scannable and easy to digest. Agent B used dense paragraphs which were harder to read.
2. **Accuracy/Width**: Agent A correctly identified the specific 'Court Shorts' video formats and durations. While Agent B correctly noted the 'Distance-Learning Module' for the second case (which Agent A listed as a video), Agent B hallucinated/over-generalized by claiming full 'distance-learning modules' with role-playing/simulations existed for the third and fourth cases (Flag Burning and Stolen Valor), which are primarily video resources. Agent A's adherence to the verifiable video resources was safer and more accurate.
